questions.text: What is the primary benefit of ordering COPY and RUN instructions carefully in a Dockerfile?
options: It makes the Dockerfile easier to read
It maximizes layer cache hits, so unchanged layers don't need to be rebuilt
It reduces the final image size
It ensures environment variables are set correctly
questions.correct: It maximizes layer cache hits, so unchanged layers don't need to be rebuilt
questions.explanation: Docker caches each layer and reuses it if the instruction and all preceding layers are unchanged. By placing rarely-changing instructions (like dependency installation) before frequently-changing ones (like source code copy), you avoid re-running expensive steps on every build.

questions.text: In a multi-stage Docker build, what happens to the intermediate build stages?
options: They are included in the final image as compressed layers
They are discarded — only the final stage becomes the output image
They are saved as separate images in the local registry
They are cached but shipped alongside the final image
questions.correct: They are discarded — only the final stage becomes the output image
questions.explanation: Intermediate stages are used only during the build process. Only the final FROM stage becomes the output image. You can selectively copy artifacts from earlier stages using 'COPY --from=stage_name', but everything else is discarded, dramatically reducing image size.

questions.text: How do services in a Docker Compose network communicate with each other?
options: Through the host machine's loopback interface (localhost)
Using the container's IP address which must be configured manually
By service name — Compose creates DNS entries for each service
Through a shared filesystem volume
questions.correct: By service name — Compose creates DNS entries for each service
questions.explanation: Docker Compose automatically creates a network and registers each service's name as a DNS entry. A service named 'postgres' is reachable at hostname 'postgres' from any other service in the same Compose network, without needing to know IP addresses.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'scratch' base image in Docker?
options: It's a minimal Linux distribution with only a shell
It's an empty image with absolutely nothing — used for statically compiled binaries
It's a security-hardened version of Alpine Linux
It's a temporary image that's automatically cleaned up
questions.correct: It's an empty image with absolutely nothing — used for statically compiled binaries
questions.explanation: The 'scratch' image is Docker's special empty image — 0 bytes. It's ideal for statically compiled languages like Go (with CGO_ENABLED=0) where the binary includes everything it needs to run. The resulting image contains only your binary and any files you explicitly COPY in.

questions.text: What does 'depends_on' with 'condition: service_healthy' ensure in Docker Compose?
options: The dependent service won't start until the dependency's health check passes
The dependent service will automatically restart if the dependency becomes unhealthy
The dependent service shares the same health check as the dependency
The dependency is rebuilt before the dependent service starts
questions.correct: The dependent service won't start until the dependency's health check passes
questions.explanation: With 'condition: service_healthy', Compose waits for the dependency's health check to report a healthy status before starting the dependent service. Without this condition, depends_on only waits for the container to start — not for the service inside it to be ready (e.g., database accepting connections).
